title: Atopic Eczema
oneLiner: Chronic inflammatory pruritic skin disease, often childhood onset, driven by barrier defect and Th2 inflammation; risk of serious skin infections and erythroderma.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Eczema herpeticum (disseminated HSV)** – *Monomorphic punched-out erosions/vesicles + fever + rapid deterioration* → this is not impetigo. If suspected: start IV aciclovir immediately, do NOT apply topical steroids alone.
- **Erythroderma (generalised exfoliative dermatitis)** – *>90% BSA erythema + scaling + systemic upset (hypothermia, high-output cardiac failure, sepsis)* → must stop all topical triggers, resuscitate, admit to HDU/ICU.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** Intense pruritus, dry skin, recurrent flare-ups.
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Erythematous plaques with scaling, excoriations, lichenification (thickened skin from chronic scratching).
  - **Flexural distribution** – antecubital, popliteal, neck, wrists, ankles (infants: face, extensor surfaces).
  - Dry skin (xerosis) and often associated signs of asthma/rhinitis (e.g. allergic shiners, Dennie-Morgan lines).
  - Secondary infection: honey-coloured crusts (bacterial impetiginisation), vesicles/erosions (viral).
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Seborrhoeic dermatitis** – greasy yellow scales on scalp, face, chest; usually less pruritic.
- **Contact dermatitis** – localised to area of exposure, e.g. nickel, fragrance; patch testing may be needed.
- **Psoriasis** – well‑circumscribed plaques, silvery scale, extensor surfaces, nail pitting, scalp thick scale.
- **Scabies** – burrows between fingers, wrists, nocturnal itch; track marks on exam.
- **Fungal skin infection** – annular, scaling edge, central clearing; KOH preparation positive.
- **Splitter:** *Flexural predominance = eczema; extensor predominance (elbows/knees) = psoriasis.*
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Skin swab for bacterial culture if crusted/weeping; viral PCR swab (or Tzanck smear) if vesicles/erosions.
- **Bloods:** IgE (elevated – non‑specific), eosinophilia; CRP/WBC to assess infection.
- **Imaging:** None routinely.
- **Special tests:** Patch testing if contact dermatitis suspected; skin biopsy for atypical cases.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** UK Working Party criteria (pruritus + ≥3 of: history of flexural involvement, visible flexural dermatitis, history of asthma/hay fever, onset <2 years, dry skin).
- **Never‑miss test:** *Viral PCR (or Tzanck smear) in any sick child/adult with widespread eczema + fever → rule out eczema herpeticum.*
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Suspected eczema herpeticum:** stop all topical steroids; take viral swab; start IV aciclovir (10 mg/kg TDS, adjusted for renal function) – do not wait for PCR result if strong suspicion. Contact dermatology on call.
  - **Erythroderma:** IV fluids resuscitation; stop all topical treatments; monitor temperature, cardiac output; involve dermatology/ICU.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Emollients:** apply liberally and frequently (every 2–4 hours) – e.g. 50/50 white soft paraffin/liquid paraffin or Diprobase.
  - **Topical corticosteroids:** choose potency based on site and severity – use fingertip unit (1 FTU ≈ 500 mg).
    - Mild (face/flexures): hydrocortisone 1% (short course 7–14 days).
    - Moderate: clobetasone butyrate 0.05%.
    - Potent: betamethasone valerate 0.1%.
    - Very potent: clobetasol propionate 0.05% (reserved for short courses on trunk/limbs, avoid face/groin).
  - **Infected eczema:** add topical antibiotic (e.g. fusidic acid with hydrocortisone – Fucidin H) for mild–moderate; oral flucloxacillin 500 mg QDS × 7 days if extensive or systemic signs.
  - **Antihistamines:** sedating (e.g. chlorphenamine) at night for itch; non‑sedating (cetirizine) if allergic rhinitis.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Regular emollients (even when clear) – maintain skin barrier.
  - Intermittent topical steroids (“step up, step down”).
  - Topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus 0.03%/0.1% or pimecrolimus) for face/flexures if steroids fail.
  - Phototherapy (narrowband UVB) for moderate–severe refractory disease.
  - Systemic agents (ciclosporin, methotrexate, azathioprine, dupilumab) – dermatology-led.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: usual maintenance (e.g. 0.9% saline + 5% glucose) if NBM or dehydrated.
  - VTE prophylaxis: consider LMWH if immobile (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD) [check local weight-based policy].
  - Diet: no restrictions unless known food trigger.
  - Observations: routine vital signs 4-hourly; if infected or erythroderma increase to hourly until stable.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - Emollients: unlimited amount, applied to damp skin.
  - Hydrocortisone 1% cream: apply 1–2 times daily × 7–14 days; then reduce frequency.
  - Clobetasone butyrate 0.05% cream: BD × 7–14 days for moderate eczema.
  - Betamethasone valerate 0.1% cream: BD × 7 days potent.
  - Clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment: OD × 7 days very potent; avoid > 2 weeks continuous.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Tacrolimus 0.03% or 0.1% ointment: apply BD for short term (check local policy for duration) – avoid sunlight, use with caution in <2 years.
  - Fusidic acid + hydrocortisone (Fucidin H) for infected eczema: apply TDS × 7 days.
  - Oral flucloxacillin: 500 mg QDS × 7 days (or clarithromycin 500 mg BD if penicillin allergic) for impetiginised eczema.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Topical steroids: avoid prolonged use on face/genitals (risk of atrophy, perioral dermatitis).
  - Tacrolimus: avoid in immunocompromised; may cause burning sensation initially.
  - Ciclosporin: check BP, renal function; multiple drug interactions (e.g. statins, NSAIDs).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Aciclovir (IV) – reduce dose if eGFR <25; check BNF.
  - Ciclosporin – adjust for hepatic impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Topical steroid withdrawal (red skin syndrome)** from abrupt cessation of potent steroids – always taper. Also: *Using very potent steroid on face → skin atrophy, rosacea-like changes, perioral dermatitis.*
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Eczema herpeticum (HSV) – requires IV aciclovir, can be fatal if untreated.
  - Impetiginisation (Strep/Staph) – treat with oral antibiotics.
  - Erythroderma – hospital admission, risk of sepsis, fluid/electrolyte loss.
- **Chronic:**
  - Skin lichenification, dyspigmentation (hypo/hyper).
  - Sleep deprivation, poor QoL, psychological distress.
  - Increased risk of asthma, allergic rhinitis (atopic march).
sections.Prognosis: - 60-70% of children outgrow by adolescence. Remaining have chronic relapsing course. Adults may have persistent flexural eczema. Mortality is extremely low; morbidity from infection or erythroderma.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Apply emollient to damp skin** after bathing (“soak and seal”) – improves absorption and reduces steroid need.
- **Use the fingertip unit (FTU)** to prescribe correct topical steroid amount (1 FTU = 500 mg; 1 FTU covers 2 palm areas).
- **For infected eczema, treat both infection and inflammation** – use combined steroid/antibiotic cream (e.g. Fucidin H) or oral antibiotic plus separate steroid.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Stopping emollients when skin clears** – emollients are maintenance therapy, not “prn”.
- **Under-treating severe eczema with weak steroids** – use adequate potency for extent/severity; if not better in 7–14 days, step up.
- **Applying potent steroids to face/flexures long-term** – limit to 7–14 days then step down.
- **Ignoring vesicles/erosions in a febrile child** – do not assume impetigo; always consider eczema herpeticum (start aciclovir early).
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Lesions become painful, weeping, crusted despite emollients.
  - Fever, drowsiness, rapid spread of rash.
  - Widespread blistering or redness > 80% BSA.
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP review in 2–4 weeks; if recurrent infections or poor control refer to dermatology.
  - For children: health visitor/paediatric review.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior/dermatology reg immediately for:** suspected eczema herpeticum, erythroderma, or severe generalised eczema with fever/systemic upset.
- **Call ICU for:** NEWS2 ≥ 5, lactate > 2, GCS < 15, respiratory distress, signs of high‑output cardiac failure (tachycardia, bounding pulses) in erythroderma.
- **Specific thresholds:** Temperature > 38.5°C with widespread rash → think HSV; escalate. NEWS2 > 3 in child → pEWS trigger.

rawMarkdown:
## Atopic Eczema
**One-liner:** Chronic inflammatory pruritic skin disease, often childhood onset, driven by barrier defect and Th2 inflammation; risk of serious skin infections and erythroderma.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Eczema herpeticum (disseminated HSV)** – *Monomorphic punched-out erosions/vesicles + fever + rapid deterioration* → this is not impetigo. If suspected: start IV aciclovir immediately, do NOT apply topical steroids alone.
- **Erythroderma (generalised exfoliative dermatitis)** – *>90% BSA erythema + scaling + systemic upset (hypothermia, high-output cardiac failure, sepsis)* → must stop all topical triggers, resuscitate, admit to HDU/ICU.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** Intense pruritus, dry skin, recurrent flare-ups.
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Erythematous plaques with scaling, excoriations, lichenification (thickened skin from chronic scratching).
  - **Flexural distribution** – antecubital, popliteal, neck, wrists, ankles (infants: face, extensor surfaces).
  - Dry skin (xerosis) and often associated signs of asthma/rhinitis (e.g. allergic shiners, Dennie-Morgan lines).
  - Secondary infection: honey-coloured crusts (bacterial impetiginisation), vesicles/erosions (viral).

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Seborrhoeic dermatitis** – greasy yellow scales on scalp, face, chest; usually less pruritic.
- **Contact dermatitis** – localised to area of exposure, e.g. nickel, fragrance; patch testing may be needed.
- **Psoriasis** – well‑circumscribed plaques, silvery scale, extensor surfaces, nail pitting, scalp thick scale.
- **Scabies** – burrows between fingers, wrists, nocturnal itch; track marks on exam.
- **Fungal skin infection** – annular, scaling edge, central clearing; KOH preparation positive.
- **Splitter:** *Flexural predominance = eczema; extensor predominance (elbows/knees) = psoriasis.*

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Skin swab for bacterial culture if crusted/weeping; viral PCR swab (or Tzanck smear) if vesicles/erosions.
- **Bloods:** IgE (elevated – non‑specific), eosinophilia; CRP/WBC to assess infection.
- **Imaging:** None routinely.
- **Special tests:** Patch testing if contact dermatitis suspected; skin biopsy for atypical cases.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** UK Working Party criteria (pruritus + ≥3 of: history of flexural involvement, visible flexural dermatitis, history of asthma/hay fever, onset <2 years, dry skin).
- **Never‑miss test:** *Viral PCR (or Tzanck smear) in any sick child/adult with widespread eczema + fever → rule out eczema herpeticum.*

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Suspected eczema herpeticum:** stop all topical steroids; take viral swab; start IV aciclovir (10 mg/kg TDS, adjusted for renal function) – do not wait for PCR result if strong suspicion. Contact dermatology on call.
  - **Erythroderma:** IV fluids resuscitation; stop all topical treatments; monitor temperature, cardiac output; involve dermatology/ICU.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Emollients:** apply liberally and frequently (every 2–4 hours) – e.g. 50/50 white soft paraffin/liquid paraffin or Diprobase.
  - **Topical corticosteroids:** choose potency based on site and severity – use fingertip unit (1 FTU ≈ 500 mg).
    - Mild (face/flexures): hydrocortisone 1% (short course 7–14 days).
    - Moderate: clobetasone butyrate 0.05%.
    - Potent: betamethasone valerate 0.1%.
    - Very potent: clobetasol propionate 0.05% (reserved for short courses on trunk/limbs, avoid face/groin).
  - **Infected eczema:** add topical antibiotic (e.g. fusidic acid with hydrocortisone – Fucidin H) for mild–moderate; oral flucloxacillin 500 mg QDS × 7 days if extensive or systemic signs.
  - **Antihistamines:** sedating (e.g. chlorphenamine) at night for itch; non‑sedating (cetirizine) if allergic rhinitis.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Regular emollients (even when clear) – maintain skin barrier.
  - Intermittent topical steroids (“step up, step down”).
  - Topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus 0.03%/0.1% or pimecrolimus) for face/flexures if steroids fail.
  - Phototherapy (narrowband UVB) for moderate–severe refractory disease.
  - Systemic agents (ciclosporin, methotrexate, azathioprine, dupilumab) – dermatology-led.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: usual maintenance (e.g. 0.9% saline + 5% glucose) if NBM or dehydrated.
  - VTE prophylaxis: consider LMWH if immobile (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD) [check local weight-based policy].
  - Diet: no restrictions unless known food trigger.
  - Observations: routine vital signs 4-hourly; if infected or erythroderma increase to hourly until stable.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - Emollients: unlimited amount, applied to damp skin.
  - Hydrocortisone 1% cream: apply 1–2 times daily × 7–14 days; then reduce frequency.
  - Clobetasone butyrate 0.05% cream: BD × 7–14 days for moderate eczema.
  - Betamethasone valerate 0.1% cream: BD × 7 days potent.
  - Clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment: OD × 7 days very potent; avoid > 2 weeks continuous.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Tacrolimus 0.03% or 0.1% ointment: apply BD for short term (check local policy for duration) – avoid sunlight, use with caution in <2 years.
  - Fusidic acid + hydrocortisone (Fucidin H) for infected eczema: apply TDS × 7 days.
  - Oral flucloxacillin: 500 mg QDS × 7 days (or clarithromycin 500 mg BD if penicillin allergic) for impetiginised eczema.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Topical steroids: avoid prolonged use on face/genitals (risk of atrophy, perioral dermatitis).
  - Tacrolimus: avoid in immunocompromised; may cause burning sensation initially.
  - Ciclosporin: check BP, renal function; multiple drug interactions (e.g. statins, NSAIDs).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Aciclovir (IV) – reduce dose if eGFR <25; check BNF.
  - Ciclosporin – adjust for hepatic impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Topical steroid withdrawal (red skin syndrome)** from abrupt cessation of potent steroids – always taper. Also: *Using very potent steroid on face → skin atrophy, rosacea-like changes, perioral dermatitis.*

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Eczema herpeticum (HSV) – requires IV aciclovir, can be fatal if untreated.
  - Impetiginisation (Strep/Staph) – treat with oral antibiotics.
  - Erythroderma – hospital admission, risk of sepsis, fluid/electrolyte loss.
- **Chronic:**
  - Skin lichenification, dyspigmentation (hypo/hyper).
  - Sleep deprivation, poor QoL, psychological distress.
  - Increased risk of asthma, allergic rhinitis (atopic march).

### Prognosis
- 60-70% of children outgrow by adolescence. Remaining have chronic relapsing course. Adults may have persistent flexural eczema. Mortality is extremely low; morbidity from infection or erythroderma.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Apply emollient to damp skin** after bathing (“soak and seal”) – improves absorption and reduces steroid need.
- **Use the fingertip unit (FTU)** to prescribe correct topical steroid amount (1 FTU = 500 mg; 1 FTU covers 2 palm areas).
- **For infected eczema, treat both infection and inflammation** – use combined steroid/antibiotic cream (e.g. Fucidin H) or oral antibiotic plus separate steroid.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Stopping emollients when skin clears** – emollients are maintenance therapy, not “prn”.
- **Under-treating severe eczema with weak steroids** – use adequate potency for extent/severity; if not better in 7–14 days, step up.
- **Applying potent steroids to face/flexures long-term** – limit to 7–14 days then step down.
- **Ignoring vesicles/erosions in a febrile child** – do not assume impetigo; always consider eczema herpeticum (start aciclovir early).

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Lesions become painful, weeping, crusted despite emollients.
  - Fever, drowsiness, rapid spread of rash.
  - Widespread blistering or redness > 80% BSA.
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP review in 2–4 weeks; if recurrent infections or poor control refer to dermatology.
  - For children: health visitor/paediatric review.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/dermatology reg immediately for:** suspected eczema herpeticum, erythroderma, or severe generalised eczema with fever/systemic upset.
- **Call ICU for:** NEWS2 ≥ 5, lactate > 2, GCS < 15, respiratory distress, signs of high‑output cardiac failure (tachycardia, bounding pulses) in erythroderma.
- **Specific thresholds:** Temperature > 38.5°C with widespread rash → think HSV; escalate. NEWS2 > 3 in child → pEWS trigger.
